metadata.title: Write a PRD Like a PM, Part 2: User Stories & Success Metrics
metadata.topic: INFO 7375 ASSIGNMENT 2 — PRD PART 2: USER STORIES & METRICS (20 PTS)
metadata.playlist: Plan It Like a PM — Your Madison PRD
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 2 brief — Plan Your Madison Project Like a Pro

beats.narration_text: E4. Two things separate a real PRD from a well-written essay about a project. First: user stories in the exact format — a precise template, not a paraphrase. Second: success metrics you can actually measure — a target, a method, a data source — not a hope. This episode covers the second 20 points of Assignment 2. We will write three user stories in the correct format, and then we will write three metrics that would tell a real PM whether the project is working.

shot.topic: Episode 4 — PRD Part 2: User Stories & Success Metrics (20 pts)
output: User Stories (10 pts): As a [role], I want [feature] so that [benefit] — 3 required
Success Metrics (10 pts): what improves · by how much · measured how

beats.narration_text: The user story format is not a suggestion — it is a template, and the rubric checks all three parts. The exact structure is: As a [role], I want [feature] so that [benefit]. Each part carries specific requirements. The role must be the specific user from your problem statement — not 'the user' in the abstract, and not 'someone.' If your problem statement named a performance marketing manager at a mid-size e-commerce brand, that is the role in your stories. The feature must name the specific thing the system does — not 'it helps me,' but 'it generates a ranked list of campaign variants sorted by predicted click-through rate.' The benefit must name the specific outcome the user gets — not 'it's faster,' but 'I can allocate budget to the highest-performing variant without waiting for a weekly report.' All three parts are required. If the 'so that' clause is missing, the story earns partial credit at best — and more importantly, you have written a feature request, not a user story. The benefit is the entire point.

shot.artifactHeading: User story template — three required parts
artifactLines: Template: As a [role], I want [feature] so that [benefit]
[role]    = the specific user from your problem statement — not 'the user'
[feature] = the specific thing the system does — not 'it helps me'
[benefit] = the specific outcome the user gets — not 'it's faster'
All three parts required — missing 'so that' = partial credit

beats.narration_text: Here is the prompt to draft your three user stories. Paste in your role and your problem statement, describe the tool you are building, and Claude will generate three stories in the correct format — each representing a distinct user need. After Claude drafts them, read each one against your problem statement. If the 'so that' clause does not connect to the specific pain you named in Episode 3, revise it before putting it on the board. The benefit has to close the loop on the problem. That is the test.

shot.topic: Draft three user stories for a Madison PRD
shot.command: I'm building a Madison-powered [DESCRIBE YOUR TOOL]. My target user is [ROLE]. My PRD problem is [ONE SENTENCE]. Write 3 user stories in the format: 'As a [role], I want [specific feature] so that [specific benefit].' Each story should represent a distinct user need — do not write three variations of the same need. After each story, flag whether the feature is achievable in a one-agent MVP or requires multiple agents.
output: Story 1: As a [role], I want [feature] so that [benefit]
Story 2: As a [role], I want [feature] so that [benefit]
Story 3: As a [role], I want [feature] so that [benefit]
Per story: one-agent MVP or multi-agent — flagged

beats.narration_text: Here is a concrete example of the format working correctly, and one where it fails. A strong story: 'As a campaign manager, I want the system to score each ad variant against past performance data so that I can allocate budget to the highest-performing variant before the next campaign cycle.' Break it down: the role is campaign manager — specific. The feature is score ad variants against past performance data — specific and technically meaningful. The benefit is allocate budget before the cycle — a real, observable outcome. Now the weak version: 'As a user, I want AI help so that things are better.' Role is not specific. Feature is not described. Benefit is not measurable. That story earns no points for specificity on any of its three parts. The difference between these two is not creativity — it is precision.

shot.artifactHeading: User story — strong vs. weak example
artifactLines: STRONG:
As a campaign manager, I want the system to score each ad variant against
past performance data so that I can allocate budget to the highest-performing
variant before the next campaign cycle.
→ role: specific · feature: specific + technical · benefit: observable
WEAK:
As a user, I want AI help so that things are better.
→ role: vague · feature: undefined · benefit: unmeasurable

beats.narration_text: Now success metrics. A metric earns credit only if it answers three questions: what improves, by how much, and how you would measure it. Each of those three is load-bearing. 'User satisfaction will improve' is not a metric — there is no target and no measurement method. 'User-reported time to generate a campaign brief drops from 45 minutes to under 15 minutes, measured via a Typeform survey administered at week 4' — that is a metric. It names the thing being measured, it gives a target, and it names the data source. Here is the prompt to draft three metrics from your user stories.

shot.topic: Draft three success metrics for a Madison PRD
shot.command: For my Madison-powered [TOOL], I have these user stories: [PASTE YOUR 3 STORIES]. Write 3 success metrics that together would tell me the project is working. For each metric: (1) name the thing being measured, (2) give a target value or improvement percentage — even a rough one, (3) name the data source or measurement method (e.g., 'time recorded in campaign dashboard,' 'error rate in A/B test logs,' 'user survey at week 4'). If a metric can't be measured with the tools in the project, flag it.
output: Flagged: any metric that cannot be measured with available tools

beats.narration_text: Three moves that push metrics from adequate to excellent. First: pull a real industry baseline. If you are measuring time saved, find a study or report that gives the current average time for that task — name the source in your PRD. A metric with a baseline is worth more than a metric with a guess. Second: name the specific tool or API you will use to collect the data. Not 'some dashboard,' but 'the n8n execution log' — and here it is worth being precise: n8n is a separate visual workflow tool, not part of Madison's architecture. Madison and n8n are different products that can be used together, but they are not the same thing. If you are using n8n to collect execution data, name it and be clear it is a workflow automation tool you are connecting to the system. Third: write one edge case. What would cause a metric to look good while the product is actually failing? For example: if the AI generates confident but wrong recommendations, the click-through rate might improve while the conversion rate drops. Track both. That is the kind of thinking that earns excellence marks.

shot.artifactHeading: Excellence — real numbers, tools, edge cases
artifactLines: 1. Industry baseline: find the current average for the task — cite the source
2. Named measurement tool: not 'some dashboard' — name the API or log
Note: n8n is a separate workflow automation tool, not part of Madison —
name it correctly if you use it for data collection
3. One edge case: what makes the metric look good when the product is failing?
Example: high click-through + low conversion → AI is confidently wrong → track both
shot.sparkLine: baseline → named tool → edge case → excellence

beats.narration_text: Four traps to avoid. Trap one: stories missing 'so that.' The benefit clause is the point of a user story. Without it, you have described a feature, not a user need, and the rubric treats them differently. Trap two: three stories for the same need. If you write 'I can view a dashboard,' 'I can see a summary,' and 'I can get an overview' — that is one need phrased three ways, not three distinct stories. Each story should represent something a different part of the user's workflow. Trap three: unmeasurable metrics. 'User satisfaction will improve' is not a metric without a scale, a target, and a collection method. Trap four: no measurement method named. 'We will track performance' means nothing. Name the tool, the log, or the API call that produces the data. The rubric rewards specificity at every level.

artifactLines: Trap 1 — Missing 'so that': you have a feature request, not a user story
Trap 2 — Three stories for one need: view / see / get overview = one need × 3
Trap 3 — Unmeasurable metrics: 'satisfaction will improve' needs scale + target + method
Trap 4 — No measurement method: 'track performance' → name the tool, log, or API
shot.sparkLine: format → distinct needs → measurable → named method

beats.narration_text: The rules for Part 2. User stories: exactly three, each in the full format — As a role, I want feature, so that benefit — and each representing a distinct user need, not three angles on the same need. Success metrics: three, each answering what improves, by how much, and how you would measure it. Excellence adds an industry baseline, a named tool or API for data collection, and one edge case that identifies when the metric could lie. Together, user stories and metrics are 20 points. And remember: the stories should connect directly to the problem statement. If the 'so that' clause does not close the loop on the pain from Episode 3, revise it.

artifactLines: User Stories (10 pts):
· Exactly 3 stories in format: As a [role], I want [feature] so that [benefit]
· Each story = a distinct user need — not 3 variations of the same need
· 'So that' must close the loop on the pain from the problem statement
Success Metrics (10 pts):
· 3 metrics: what improves · by how much · how measured
· Each metric needs a named data source or tool — not 'some dashboard'
Excellence: industry baseline + named API/tool + one edge case per metric

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Take the feature ideas you have in mind — the things you are imagining the system doing — and paste them into this prompt. Claude will convert them into correctly formatted user stories and propose one measurable metric for each, with a target and a measurement method. Then read the output. If any story's benefit is vague, revise it. If any metric lacks a measurement method, add one. This is the conversion step — from 'here is what I think the system should do' to 'here is a PRD section that earns points.' Do this before you build anything on the Figma board for this section.

shot.topic: Convert feature ideas to user stories and metrics
shot.segment: Before you build — convert and check
shot.command: Here are my project's feature ideas: [LIST YOUR IDEAS]. My target user is [ROLE]. My PRD problem: [ONE SENTENCE]. Convert each idea into a user story in format 'As a [role], I want [feature] so that [benefit].' Then propose one success metric for each story — with a target value and a named measurement method. Flag any story where the benefit is vague or any metric where the measurement method is unclear.
shot.runningText: converting feature ideas to user stories and metrics…
output: User story per idea — full format with role · feature · benefit
One metric per story — target value + named measurement method
Flagged: vague benefits · unclear measurement methods
